phase1.explanation:
The Collections Framework provides a unified architecture for storing and manipulating groups of objects.

Think of collections like different types of containers:
• List = an ordered list (like a shopping list)
• Set = unique items only (like a set of dinner plates)
• Map = key-value pairs (like a dictionary)
• Queue = FIFO processing (like a line at a store)

--- Collection Hierarchy ---

Iterable (root)
    └── Collection
        ├── List
        │   ├── ArrayList
        │   ├── LinkedList
        │   └── Vector (legacy)
        ├── Set
        │   ├── HashSet
        │   ├── TreeSet
        │   └── LinkedHashSet
        └── Queue
            ├── LinkedList
            └── PriorityQueue

Map (separate hierarchy)
    ├── HashMap
    ├── TreeMap
    └── LinkedHashMap

--- When to Use Which ---

ArrayList:
• Fast random access by index
• Fast iteration
• Good for: storing and accessing data by position

LinkedList:
• Fast insertion/removal at beginning/end
• Good for: implementing stacks, queues

HashSet:
• Fast lookup, no duplicates
• No order guaranteed
• Good for: removing duplicates, membership testing

TreeSet:
• Sorted order
• Good for: sorted unique elements

HashMap:
• Fast key-value lookup
• No order guaranteed
• Good for: dictionaries, caches, counts

TreeMap:
• Sorted keys
• Good for: sorted key-value pairs

--- Key Interfaces ---

Collection:
• Basic operations: add, remove, contains, size, isEmpty

List:
• Ordered collection, allows duplicates
• Index-based access: get(index), set(index, element)

Set:
• No duplicates, no order (usually)
• Based on equals() and hashCode()

Map:
• Key-value pairs, unique keys
• put(key, value), get(key), containsKey(key)

Queue:
• FIFO (First-In-First-Out)
• offer(), poll(), peek()

--- Choosing the Right Collection ---

Ask yourself:
1. Do I need duplicates? → List (yes) / Set (no)
2. Do I need order? → List, TreeSet, LinkedHashMap
3. Do I need fast lookup? → HashMap, HashSet
4. Do I need sorting? → TreeSet, TreeMap
5. Do I need FIFO? → Queue

Pro tip: Use the most general interface (List, Set, Map) in your code, not the specific implementation!

phase2.explanation:
Conceptual lesson — no broken code!

Common misconception: "I should always use ArrayList because it's the most popular."

Actually: ArrayList isn't always the best choice! If you're frequently inserting/deleting at the beginning of a list, LinkedList would be more efficient.

Example:
• ArrayList: O(1) for get, O(n) for insert at beginning
• LinkedList: O(n) for get, O(1) for insert at beginning

Choose based on your specific use case!

Another misconception: "HashSet is faster than TreeSet."

Actually: HashSet is O(1) for operations, TreeSet is O(log n). If you need sorted order, TreeSet is your only choice, but if you don't need order, HashSet is faster.

phase3.prompt:
Which collection should you use for:

"A list of students where you frequently need to access students by their ID number"

A) ArrayList
B) LinkedList
C) HashSet
D) HashMap

Select the BEST choice.
dialogueHints: Think about what you're using to access the data...
phase3.openingDialogue: Collections — choose the right container for the job! 📦
